parentQuestion: Postganglionic sympathetic fibers to sweat glands secrete acetylcholine (ACh), not norepinephrine.

scaffolds.q: Sweating is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sweating increases during stress/exercise - that's sympathetic 'fight or flight' preparing for heat dissipation.

scaffolds.q: Most sympathetic postganglionic fibers release norepinephrine (NE).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Heart, blood vessels, pupils - most sympathetic targets receive NE. But there are exceptions.

scaffolds.q: Sweat glands are the major EXCEPTION to the 'sympathetic = NE' rule.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Even though sweat glands are sympathetically controlled, the postganglionic fibers release ACh, not NE.

scaffolds.q: Sweat glands have muscarinic receptors, not adrenergic receptors.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Muscarinic receptors respond to ACh. That's why atropine (muscarinic blocker) inhibits sweating.

scaffolds.q: Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors and causes dry skin (reduced sweating).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Even though sweating is sympathetic, atropine blocks it because sweat glands use ACh/muscarinic signaling.

scaffolds.q: Beta-blockers (adrenergic blockers) do NOT affect sweating.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Sweat glands don't have adrenergic receptors, so beta-blockers don't touch them. Wrong receptor type.

scaffolds.q: Sympathetic but cholinergic = sweat glands (the famous exception).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This exception appears on exams constantly. Know it: Sympathetic innervation, but ACh/muscarinic transmission.

scaffolds.q: Sympathetic fibers to sweat glands release ACh, not NE.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The sympathetic cholinergic exception. Sweating is sympathetically controlled but uses cholinergic transmission.

scaffolds.q: The statement is TRUE - sweat gland innervation is sympathetic but cholinergic.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The answer is TRUE. This is the most important sympathetic exception: Sweat glands receive sympathetic fibers that release ACh onto muscarinic receptors.
